questions.question: Explain the photovoltaic effect in solar cells.
keyPoints: Photovoltaic effect is the generation of voltage and electric current when semiconductor materials are exposed to sunlight
When photons strike the semiconductor, they knock electrons loose from their atoms
This creates free electrons that flow through the material, generating electric current
The process converts light energy directly into electrical energy without any mechanical intermediate step

questions.question: List and explain three main types of solar PV cells with their efficiencies.
keyPoints: Monocrystalline silicon: ~25% efficiency, made from single crystal silicon, most efficient but expensive
Polycrystalline silicon: ~20% efficiency, made from melted silicon fragments, less efficient but cheaper
Thin-film: ~10-13% efficiency, uses materials like cadmium telluride, least efficient but flexible and lightweight

questions.question: What is net metering? Explain its importance in solar energy systems.
keyPoints: Net metering is a billing mechanism that credits solar system owners for excess electricity they feed into the grid
When solar panels generate more than needed, the excess goes to the grid
During nighttime or low production, electricity is drawn from the grid
This allows users to offset their electricity bills and promotes grid integration of renewable energy

questions.question: Describe the working of a solar water heating system.
keyPoints: Solar water heaters use collectors (flat-plate or evacuated tubes) to absorb sunlight
The absorbed heat is transferred to water or a heat transfer fluid circulating through the collector
Heated water is stored in an insulated tank for later use
A backup heater may be used during low sunlight periods for continuous hot water supply

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of solar energy?
keyPoints: Advantages: renewable, inexhaustible, environmentally friendly (no emissions), low operating costs, low maintenance, scalable
Disadvantages: high initial cost, weather-dependent, requires large space, intermittent supply, efficiency losses during conversion
Despite disadvantages, solar is a key renewable energy source for sustainable development

questions.question: Explain the concept of solar insolation and its importance.
keyPoints: Solar insolation is the amount of solar radiation received per unit area, measured in kWh/m²/day
It varies based on geographic location, season, time of day, and weather conditions
Higher insolation areas are more suitable for solar installations
It helps in designing solar systems and predicting energy output

questions.question: What is Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)? Explain its working principle.
keyPoints: CSP uses mirrors or lenses to concentrate a large area of sunlight onto a small focal point
This concentration generates high temperatures (300-1000°C)
The heat is used to produce steam that drives turbines to generate electricity
It is different from photovoltaic systems as it uses thermal conversion instead of direct electrical conversion

questions.question: Explain the working of a grid-connected solar PV system with battery storage. Draw a block diagram and explain the function of each component.
keyPoints: Block diagram showing: Solar Panels → DC Bus → Charge Controller → Battery Bank → Inverter → AC Bus → Grid/Home Load
Solar Panels: Convert sunlight to DC electricity using photovoltaic effect
Charge Controller: Regulates voltage and current to prevent battery overcharging
Battery Bank: Stores excess electricity for use during nighttime or low production periods
Inverter: Converts DC from panels/batteries to AC for home use and grid connection
Grid: Provides backup power when solar insufficient, and accepts excess power via net metering
Protection devices: Include disconnects, fuses, and surge protectors for safety
This hybrid system ensures continuous power supply regardless of weather conditions

questions.question: Discuss the factors affecting the efficiency of solar PV systems and suggest methods to maximize energy capture.
keyPoints: Factors affecting efficiency: Solar irradiance, panel temperature, shading, dust/dirt, panel angle and orientation, inverter efficiency, wiring losses

To maximize capture: Use high-efficiency panels (monocrystalline), install at optimal angle (latitude-based), implement solar tracking systems, minimize shading, regular cleaning, use MPPT charge controllers, proper inverter sizing
Temperature: Higher temperatures reduce PV efficiency (~0.5% per °C above 25°C), so cooling methods can help
Location selection: Areas with high solar insolation (>5 kWh/m²/day) are ideal
System design: Proper string sizing, good wiring practices, and quality components reduce losses

questions.question: Describe various applications of solar energy in modern society. Explain how solar technology contributes to sustainable development.
keyPoints: Applications: Solar PV for electricity (rooftop, utility-scale), solar water heating, solar-powered lighting, solar pumps for agriculture, solar charging stations for EVs, solar-powered communication systems, solar desalination
Solar energy reduces carbon footprint and greenhouse gas emissions
It decreases dependence on fossil fuels and imported energy
Creates jobs in manufacturing, installation, and maintenance sectors
Provides energy access to remote areas without grid connectivity
Supports rural development through solar microgrids
Contributes to climate change mitigation goals per Paris Agreement
Enables decentralized energy generation, improving energy security

questions.question: Explain the difference between solar photovoltaic (PV) systems and solar thermal systems. Discuss their respective applications.
keyPoints: Solar PV: Converts sunlight directly to electricity using semiconductors (photovoltaic effect), produces DC power, used for general electrical needs
Solar Thermal: Converts sunlight to heat using collectors, heats water or air for thermal applications
PV Applications: Rooftop power generation, solar farms, portable chargers, space missions, calculators
Solar Thermal Applications: Domestic water heating, space heating, pool heating, industrial process heat, concentrated solar power plants
Efficiency comparison: PV ~15-25%, Thermal ~50-80% for water heating
Cost comparison: PV has higher initial cost but versatile; thermal cheaper for specific heating applications
Hybrid systems can combine both for optimal energy utilization
